This explores the upper bound of polarity effects on surface tension and solvation energy while maintaining liquid stability through enhanced cohesion.

proposal.reasoning: Based on the diagnostic report, we have three critical issues: (1) Density underestimated by 8% and HVap by 11% indicating insufficient cohesive energy/packing, (2) Dipole moment underestimated by 32% requiring increased charge separation, and (3) Surface tension overestimated by 28% suggesting conflicting interfacial behavior. Since we're in exploration phase with recent improvement (score dropped from 234 to 78.8), I will propose two orthogonal strategies: Fork 1 prioritizes maximizing the dipole moment by pushing charges to the boundary (±0.25) and dummy bond lengths to maximum (1.25 Å) while slightly reducing epsilon to mitigate surface tension increases from enhanced polarity. Fork 2 prioritizes maximizing density and HVap by minimizing Rmin values (4.7/2.65) and increasing epsilon magnitude (-0.58/-0.68) for stronger cohesion, with moderate charge increases (±0.20) to partially address dipole without compromising stability. Both forks maintain high force constants (145, 35) to prevent crashes with increased electrostatics or tighter packing.

Fork 2 minimizes Rmin to 4.7/2.65 (5-10% reduction as recommended) and increases epsilon magnitude to -0.58/-0.68 to enhance packing density and cohesive energy, directly targeting the 8% density and 11% HVap deficits through improved van der Waals interactions rather than electrostatics.

Fork 2 will improve density toward 1.095 g/cm³ and HVap toward 12.65 kcal/mol by optimizing packing efficiency (smaller Rmin) and cohesive strength (more negative epsilon), testing if tighter packing can resolve bulk property deficits without exacerbating surface tension issues.
hypothesis.test_method: Both forks will be evaluated in parallel MD simulations at 298K and 323K, monitoring density, heat of vaporization, surface tension, and dipole moment. Success requires stable simulations (no velocity explosions) and improved agreement with target properties, particularly testing whether Fork 1's electrostatic approach or Fork 2's packing approach better resolves the conflicting property requirements.
